---
title: "Bringing Work Trips Closer to Home"
subtitle: "Wasatch Front Regional Travel Demand Model — Sketch-Level HBW Trip Redistribution Test"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Contents"
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
jupyter: july2025
---

```{python}
#| label: setup

import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
import os
import sys

sys.path.insert(0, os.path.join('..', '..'))
from chart_utils import figure_with_shift_toggle, styled_table_with_toggle, CHART_CONFIG

# All data loading/aggregation lives in
# run_sets/bring-work-trips-closer-to-home/report_loader.py, shared with
# slides.qmd. It reads from a frozen snapshot once this run set is retired
# (tdmruns snapshot-run-set / purge-run-set-outputs), falling back to
# whatever tdmruns import-manual-run most recently curated under runs/
# otherwise. See CLAUDE.md and this run set's REPORT_PLAN.md.
sys.path.insert(0, os.path.join('..', '..', '..', 'run_sets', 'bring-work-trips-closer-to-home'))
import report_loader as loader

frames = loader.load()
scenario_ids = frames['scenario_ids']
scenario_meta = frames['scenario_meta']
corridor_volumes = frames['corridor_volumes']
freeway_by_county = frames['freeway_by_county']
corridor_orientation = frames['corridor_orientation']
vmt_vhd = frames['vmt_vhd_by_county_facility']
congested_miles = frames['congested_miles']
vht_hh = frames['vht_per_household']
transit = frames['transit_ridership']
trip_length = frames['hbw_trip_length']
trip_length_dist = frames['hbw_trip_length_distribution']
trip_length_avg = frames['hbw_trip_length_weighted_average']
city_results = frames['city_results']
mode_share = frames['mode_share']

GEO_ORDER = loader.GEO_ORDER  # City Area, Medium District, Workshop Area (City Area leads)
GEO_COLORS = {'City Area': '#2a78d6', 'Medium District': '#1baf7a', 'Workshop Area': '#eda100'}
COUNTY_ORDER = ['Salt Lake', 'Davis', 'Utah', 'Weber', 'Box Elder', 'Region']
CITY_ORDER = sorted(loader.TARGET_CITIES.keys())
TRANSIT_LINE_ORDER = ['FrontRunner', 'TRAX Blue Line', 'TRAX Green Line', 'TRAX Red Line', loader.BUS_LABEL]
PERIOD_ORDER = loader.PERIOD_ORDER  # ['Peak', 'Off-Peak', 'Daily']

# Most charts below carry every shift level that's landed (5%/10%/25%) at
# once, with buttons (chart_utils.figure_with_shift_toggle) to pick which
# one is visible -- a static Quarto render has no backend, so "toggle"
# means "pre-build every shift level's traces and let Plotly's own
# updatemenus show/hide them client-side," not a real parameter callback.
# Plain-language narrative numbers and every table stay fixed at one shift
# level/period instead (tables are pandas-styled dataframes, not Plotly
# figures, so the same visibility-toggle trick doesn't apply to them --
# they get their own Absolute/% Change toggle, chart_utils.
# styled_table_with_toggle, instead) -- both explicitly filtered to
# PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT rather than left to implicitly (and wrongly) sum every
# shift level together.
PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT = 10
```

**[← All run sets](../../index.qmd)** &nbsp;|&nbsp; [Slides view](slides.html)

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
from IPython.display import Markdown, HTML, display
byline = loader.rd.run_set_byline(loader.RUN_SET_ID)
if byline:
    display(Markdown(f"*{byline}*"))
```

## The Question

What would it look like, in rough terms, if more people who live in an area of our region also worked in that area — instead of commuting elsewhere? This sketch-level test asks: if a share of home-based-work (HBW) trips that currently start inside a target area but end outside it instead ended inside the target area — following today's existing internal destination pattern — what would change at the corridor and regional level?

Three geography types are tested, each applied region-wide (every area of that type shifted simultaneously): **City Area** (roughly a single incorporated city boundary, ~80 areas region-wide), **Medium District** (a mid-sized planning district, coarser than city boundaries, often spanning parts of one or more cities or unincorporated areas — ~48 areas region-wide), and **Workshop Area** (the same city groupings used for our public workshops — ~12 region-wide). Per the approved scoping memo, **City Area is the lead geography for this write-up**; Medium District and Workshop Area are shown throughout as a robustness check across scales. The model basis is the **2027 RTP adoption year** network and land use, model version **v10.0.0-beta.1**.

This is not a land-use forecast. It is a directional, order-of-magnitude check on whether "closer jobs-housing balance" is worth studying further — see [Caveats](#caveats-and-interpretation).

---

## Plain-Language Takeaways

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

def _row(geo):
    v = vmt_vhd[(vmt_vhd['CO_NAME'] == 'Region') & (vmt_vhd['geography_label'] == geo) & (vmt_vhd['shift_pct'] == PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT) & (vmt_vhd['period'] == 'Peak')]
    vhd, base_vhd = v['delta_DY_VHD'].sum(), v['base_DY_VHD'].sum()
    vmt, base_vmt = v['delta_DY_VMT'].sum(), v['base_DY_VMT'].sum()
    hh_rows = vht_hh[(vht_hh['CO_NAME'] == 'Region') & (vht_hh['geography_label'] == geo) & (vht_hh['shift_pct'] == PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT) & (vht_hh['period'] == 'Peak')].iloc[0]
    hh, base_hh = hh_rows['delta_VHT_PER_HH'], hh_rows['base_VHT_PER_HH']
    return vhd, base_vhd, vmt, base_vmt, hh, base_hh

city_vhd, base_city_vhd, city_vmt, base_city_vmt, city_hh, base_city_hh = _row('City Area')
pct_city_vhd = city_vhd / base_city_vhd * 100
pct_city_vmt = city_vmt / base_city_vmt * 100
pct_city_hh = city_hh / base_city_hh * 100

# Funding/prioritization-oriented figures below draw on the same City Area,
# 10%-shift, Peak-period slice as the rest of this section -- the corridor
# with the largest Peak VHD reduction (Corridor Detail's own ranking) and
# the region's severely-congested (V/C > 1.0) Peak-period lane-miles
# (Severe Congestion section), since funding/prioritization audiences care
# more about which specific facilities are stressed than the region-wide
# average.
top_corr_row = corridor_volumes[
    (corridor_volumes['geography_label'] == 'City Area') & (corridor_volumes['shift_pct'] == PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT)
    & (corridor_volumes['period'] == 'Peak')
].sort_values('delta_DY_VHD').iloc[0]

cong_primary = congested_miles[
    (congested_miles['CO_NAME'] == 'Region') & (congested_miles['geography_label'] == 'City Area')
    & (congested_miles['shift_pct'] == PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT) & (congested_miles['period'] == 'Peak')
]
cong_base = cong_primary['base_congested_miles'].sum()
cong_delta = cong_primary['delta_congested_miles'].sum()

transit_primary = transit[(transit['geography_label'] == 'City Area') & (transit['shift_pct'] == PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT)]
transit_delta = transit_primary['delta_Boardings'].sum()
transit_base = transit_primary['base_Boardings'].sum()
pct_transit = transit_delta / transit_base * 100

# Scale-of-mechanism number: how many baseline daily HBW trips actually
# get redistributed at this shift level, and what share of all HBW trips
# that represents -- see compute_city_area_shift_trips()'s own docstring
# for the zone-numbering assumption this estimate rests on (footnoted
# below and in the Appendix).
trip_shift = loader.compute_city_area_shift_trips(PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT)

display(Markdown(
    f"- At a **{PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT}% shift**, **City Area** redistribution reduces region-wide Peak-period "
    f"vehicle-hours of delay (VHD) by roughly **{abs(city_vhd):,.0f} hours/day ({abs(pct_city_vhd):.1f}%)** and "
    f"Peak-period VMT by roughly **{abs(city_vmt):,.0f} miles/day ({abs(pct_city_vmt):.1f}%)** — a small fraction "
    "of regional totals, but directionally consistent with what the mechanism should produce "
    "(see [Congestion](#congestion-vehicle-hours-of-delay) and [Why VHD Moved](#why-vhd-moved-vehicle-miles-traveled)).\n"
    f"- At a **{PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT}% shift**, roughly **{trip_shift['shifted_trips']:,.0f} HBW trips/day** are "
    f"redistributed region-wide — about **{trip_shift['pct_of_total']:.1f}% of all {trip_shift['total_trips']:,.0f} "
    "daily HBW trips** in the region — out of the roughly "
    f"{trip_shift['eligible_trips'] / trip_shift['total_trips'] * 100:.0f}% of HBW trips that start inside a City "
    "Area and end outside it in the baseline (a rough, order-of-magnitude estimate — see the Appendix).\n"
    f"- Peak-period vehicle-hours of travel per household falls by about **{abs(city_hh)*60:.1f} minutes/day "
    f"({abs(pct_city_hh):.1f}%)** region-wide under the City Area scenario — the closest read on \"time given back "
    "to households\" (see [Free Time Added Back](#free-time-added-back)).\n"
    f"- **Transit ridership on TRAX, FrontRunner, and bus all decline slightly** under all three geography types — "
    f"about **{abs(transit_delta):,.1f} fewer daily boardings ({abs(pct_transit):.1f}%)** region-wide under City "
    "Area — shortening commutes reduces the number of trips long enough for transit to compete on, an "
    "important, non-obvious side effect (see [Transit Ridership](#transit-ridership)).\n"
    "- Medium District and Workshop Area shifts produce **similar-direction, somewhat smaller** reductions "
    "at this shift level — see the [cross-scale comparison](#cross-scale-comparison).\n"
    "\n**For funding and capacity-prioritization decisions**, which tend to focus on the Peak period and on "
    "corridors already near or over capacity:\n\n"
    f"- The Peak-period VHD reduction is not spread evenly — it concentrates on the region's already most "
    f"heavily-traveled, most delay-prone corridors. **{top_corr_row['corridor_label']}** sees the largest single-corridor "
    f"Peak VHD reduction (**{abs(top_corr_row['delta_DY_VHD']):,.0f} hours/day ({abs(top_corr_row['delta_DY_VHD'] / top_corr_row['base_DY_VHD'] * 100):.1f}%)**) "
    "under the City Area scenario — see [Corridor Detail](#corridor-detail).\n"
    f"- Peak-period **miles of severely congested roadway (V/C > 1.0) fall by about {abs(cong_delta):,.1f} miles "
    f"({abs(cong_delta / cong_base * 100):.1f}%)** region-wide (from **{cong_base:,.1f} miles** today) under the "
    "City Area scenario — a length-based read on how much less roadway crosses into failing conditions during the "
    "commute peaks, distinct from delay/VMT and more directly comparable to how capacity-expansion needs are "
    "typically scoped — see [Severe Congestion](#severe-congestion).\n"
    "- Because the mechanism only touches HBW destinations (not capacity or land use), it doesn't substitute for "
    "planned roadway investment — it's a directional read on how much *demand-side* pressure a "
    "closer jobs-housing balance could take off the same corridors that funding decisions already target "
    "(see [Caveats](#caveats-and-interpretation)).\n"
    "- Most charts below let you **toggle between shift levels**, and the VMT/VHD/VHT charts also toggle "
    "between **Peak, Off-Peak, and Daily** periods — "
    "see [Caveats](#caveats-and-interpretation) before drawing conclusions regardless of level."
))
```

---

## How the Test Works {#how-the-test-works}

Home-based-work trips starting inside a target area but ending outside it are identified in the model's trip table. A share of those trips (5%, 10%, or 25%) is proportionally redistributed to end inside the target area instead, following the *existing* internal destination pattern (a location that already attracts 15% of work trips into the area gets 15% of the newly shifted trips too). This redistribution is applied to the HBW destination-choice matrix **before mode choice**; everything downstream (mode choice, assignment) runs normally on the adjusted trip pattern. Everything else about the region — total trips, population, other destinations — is unchanged.

---

## Big Picture: Region-Wide Effect by Shift Level {#big-picture}

The detailed charts below let you toggle between shift levels one at a time. These two are the opposite: every shift level shown **at once**, side by side, region-wide, Peak period only — so the overall size of the effect (and how it scales with shift size) is visible at a glance, without clicking through options.

```{python}
#| label: fig-big-picture-vmt-vhd

SHIFT_COLORS = {'5%': '#a6cee3', '10%': '#3182bd', '25%': '#08306b'}

region_shift = vmt_vhd[
    (vmt_vhd['CO_NAME'] == 'Region') & (vmt_vhd['scenario_id'] != loader.BASELINE_SCENARIO) & (vmt_vhd['period'] == 'Peak')
].groupby(['geography_label', 'shift_pct'], as_index=False)[['delta_DY_VMT', 'base_DY_VMT', 'delta_DY_VHD', 'base_DY_VHD']].sum()
region_shift['pct_DY_VMT'] = region_shift['delta_DY_VMT'] / region_shift['base_DY_VMT'] * 100
region_shift['pct_DY_VHD'] = region_shift['delta_DY_VHD'] / region_shift['base_DY_VHD'] * 100
region_shift['shift_label'] = region_shift['shift_pct'].apply(lambda v: f'{int(v)}%')
shift_label_order = [f'{int(s)}%' for s in sorted(region_shift['shift_pct'].unique())]

def _big_picture(y_col, y_title, title):
    fig = px.bar(
        region_shift, x='geography_label', y=y_col, color='shift_label',
        barmode='group',
        category_orders={'geography_label': GEO_ORDER, 'shift_label': shift_label_order},
        color_discrete_map=SHIFT_COLORS,
        labels={'geography_label': '', y_col: y_title, 'shift_label': 'Shift Level'},
    )
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color='#c3c2b7', line_width=1)
    for trace in fig.data:
        if trace.hovertemplate:
            trace.hovertemplate = trace.hovertemplate.replace('%{y}', '%{y:+,.3r}%')
    fig.update_layout(
        title=dict(text=title, font_size=15),
        height=420, margin=dict(t=80, b=60),
        yaxis=dict(tickformat='.1f', ticksuffix='%'),
    )
    return fig

_big_picture('pct_DY_VHD', 'Change in VHD (%), Region', 'Region-Wide VHD Change, All Shift Levels<br><sup>Peak period, % of baseline</sup>').show(config=CHART_CONFIG)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-big-picture-vmt

_big_picture('pct_DY_VMT', 'Change in VMT (%), Region', 'Region-Wide VMT Change, All Shift Levels<br><sup>Peak period, % of baseline</sup>').show(config=CHART_CONFIG)
```

Both metrics scale with shift size in a straightforward, roughly proportional way across all three geography types — a useful sanity check before diving into the county/corridor/facility-type detail below, where the shift-level toggle takes over.

---

## Congestion: Vehicle-Hours of Delay {#congestion-vehicle-hours-of-delay}

This is the headline metric — congestion is what people actually feel, more than raw traffic volume. Vehicle-hours of delay (VHD) is summed across all facility types and shown by county and region, for all three geography types. Use the buttons to switch shift levels.

```{python}
#| label: fig-vhd-county

vhd_df = vmt_vhd[vmt_vhd['scenario_id'] != loader.BASELINE_SCENARIO].copy()
vhd_df['CO_NAME'] = pd.Categorical(vhd_df['CO_NAME'], categories=COUNTY_ORDER, ordered=True)
vhd_by_county = vhd_df.groupby(['CO_NAME', 'geography_label', 'shift_pct', 'period'], as_index=False, observed=True)[['delta_DY_VHD', 'base_DY_VHD']].sum()
vhd_by_county['pct_DY_VHD'] = vhd_by_county['delta_DY_VHD'] / vhd_by_county['base_DY_VHD'] * 100

def _build_vhd_county(sub, y_col='delta_DY_VHD'):
    fig = px.bar(
        sub, x='CO_NAME', y=y_col, color='geography_label',
        barmode='group',
        category_orders={'CO_NAME': COUNTY_ORDER, 'geography_label': GEO_ORDER},
        color_discrete_map=GEO_COLORS,
        labels={'CO_NAME': 'County', y_col: 'Change in VHD (hours/day)', 'geography_label': 'Geography Type'},
    )
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color='#c3c2b7', line_width=1)
    return fig

fig = figure_with_shift_toggle(
    vhd_by_county, _build_vhd_county, pct_col='pct_DY_VHD',
    value_axis_title='Change in VHD (hours/day)', pct_axis_title='Change in VHD (%)',
    period_col='period', default_period='Peak', period_order=PERIOD_ORDER,
)
fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text='Change in Vehicle-Hours of Delay vs. Baseline<br><sup>All facility types</sup>', font_size=15),
    height=460, margin=dict(t=80),
    legend_title_text='Geography Type',
)
fig.show(config=CHART_CONFIG)
```

Salt Lake and Utah counties see the largest absolute VHD reductions under every geography type, consistent with where the region's congestion (and therefore its redistribution opportunity) is concentrated. **City Area** produces the largest region-wide VHD reduction of the three geography types, though the gap to Medium District is small.

### By Facility Type

```{python}
#| label: fig-vhd-facility

vhd_ft_df = vmt_vhd[(vmt_vhd['CO_NAME'] == 'Region') & (vmt_vhd['scenario_id'] != loader.BASELINE_SCENARIO)].copy()
vhd_ft_df['pct_DY_VHD'] = vhd_ft_df['delta_DY_VHD'] / vhd_ft_df['base_DY_VHD'] * 100
ft_order = vhd_ft_df[vhd_ft_df['period'] == 'Peak'].groupby('FTCLASS')['delta_DY_VHD'].sum().sort_values().index.tolist()

def _build_vhd_facility(sub, y_col='delta_DY_VHD'):
    fig = px.bar(
        sub, x='FTCLASS', y=y_col, color='geography_label',
        barmode='group',
        category_orders={'FTCLASS': ft_order, 'geography_label': GEO_ORDER},
        color_discrete_map=GEO_COLORS,
        labels={'FTCLASS': 'Facility Type', y_col: 'Change in VHD (hours/day), Region', 'geography_label': 'Geography Type'},
    )
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color='#c3c2b7', line_width=1)
    fig.update_layout(xaxis_tickangle=-15)
    return fig

fig = figure_with_shift_toggle(
    vhd_ft_df, _build_vhd_facility, pct_col='pct_DY_VHD',
    value_axis_title='Change in VHD (hours/day), Region', pct_axis_title='Change in VHD (%), Region',
    period_col='period', default_period='Peak', period_order=PERIOD_ORDER,
)
fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text='Region-Wide Change in VHD by Facility Type', font_size=15),
    height=460, margin=dict(t=80),
    legend_title_text='Geography Type',
)
fig.show(config=CHART_CONFIG)
```

Freeways carry most of the region's delay reduction in absolute terms — expected, since that's where most of the region's VHD sits today. Arterial and collector reductions are smaller but consistent in direction.

---

## Why VHD Moved: Vehicle Miles Traveled {#why-vhd-moved-vehicle-miles-traveled}

VMT is explanatory here, not the headline: shorter commutes mean less driving, and less driving on congested facilities is why delay falls.

```{python}
#| label: fig-vmt-county

vmt_by_county = vhd_df.groupby(['CO_NAME', 'geography_label', 'shift_pct', 'period'], as_index=False, observed=True)[['delta_DY_VMT', 'base_DY_VMT']].sum()
vmt_by_county['pct_DY_VMT'] = vmt_by_county['delta_DY_VMT'] / vmt_by_county['base_DY_VMT'] * 100

def _build_vmt_county(sub, y_col='delta_DY_VMT'):
    fig = px.bar(
        sub, x='CO_NAME', y=y_col, color='geography_label',
        barmode='group',
        category_orders={'CO_NAME': COUNTY_ORDER, 'geography_label': GEO_ORDER},
        color_discrete_map=GEO_COLORS,
        labels={'CO_NAME': 'County', y_col: 'Change in VMT (miles/day)', 'geography_label': 'Geography Type'},
    )
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color='#c3c2b7', line_width=1)
    return fig

fig = figure_with_shift_toggle(
    vmt_by_county, _build_vmt_county, pct_col='pct_DY_VMT',
    value_axis_title='Change in VMT (miles/day)', pct_axis_title='Change in VMT (%)',
    period_col='period', default_period='Peak', period_order=PERIOD_ORDER,
)
fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text='Change in Vehicle Miles Traveled vs. Baseline<br><sup>All facility types</sup>', font_size=15),
    height=460, margin=dict(t=80),
    legend_title_text='Geography Type',
)
fig.show(config=CHART_CONFIG)
```

Region-wide, the shift reduces daily VMT across all geography types — City Area and Medium District produce similar-sized reductions; Workshop Area's is somewhat smaller, consistent with it being the coarsest geography (fewer, larger areas mean some "shifted" trips were already relatively close by).

---

## VMT vs. VHT/Household by City {#vmt-vs-vhd}

Each point is one of the ten target cities' change in daily HBW VMT (x-axis) plotted against its change in Peak-period VHT per household (y-axis), both shown as a percent of that city's own baseline, colored by county — a way to see whether "less driving" and "time given back to households" track together at the city level, not just region-wide. Points in the lower-left quadrant (less VMT, less VHT/HH) are the expected pattern. **City Area scenario only** — VHT/HH is only computed per-city under City Area, the one geography type whose redistribution boundaries are these same cities (Medium District/Workshop Area use different, larger target areas and aren't shown here); VHD itself (vehicle-hours of delay) isn't included since it's only available at the county level (a link-level network measure, not a per-city one — see the Appendix). Both axes stay fixed across every shift level so switching levels doesn't rescale the plot.

```{python}
#| label: fig-vmt-vs-vhd

scatter_df = city_results[city_results['scenario_id'] != loader.BASELINE_SCENARIO].copy()
scatter_df['pct_VMT'] = scatter_df['delta_VMT'] / scatter_df['base_VMT'] * 100
scatter_df['pct_VHT_PER_HH'] = scatter_df['delta_VHT_PER_HH'] / scatter_df['base_VHT_PER_HH'] * 100
COUNTY_COLORS = {'Salt Lake': '#2a78d6', 'Davis': '#1baf7a', 'Utah': '#eda100', 'Weber': '#d6604d', 'Box Elder': '#8a6fbf'}
CITY_COUNTY_ORDER = sorted(scatter_df['CO_NAME'].unique(), key=lambda c: COUNTY_ORDER.index(c))

def _build_vmt_vs_vhd(sub):
    fig = px.scatter(
        sub, x='pct_VMT', y='pct_VHT_PER_HH', color='CO_NAME', text='city_label',
        category_orders={'CO_NAME': CITY_COUNTY_ORDER},
        color_discrete_map=COUNTY_COLORS,
        labels={'pct_VMT': 'Δ Daily HBW VMT (%)', 'pct_VHT_PER_HH': 'Δ Peak VHT/HH (%)', 'CO_NAME': 'County'},
    )
    fig.update_traces(marker=dict(size=13, line=dict(width=1, color='white')), textposition='top center')
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color='#c3c2b7', line_width=1)
    fig.add_vline(x=0, line_color='#c3c2b7', line_width=1)
    return fig

fig = figure_with_shift_toggle(scatter_df, _build_vmt_vs_vhd, numeric_x=True)

# figure_with_shift_toggle only auto-fixes the y-axis range across shift
# levels (built for bar charts, where x is categorical) -- this chart's
# x-axis is also a continuous value, so it needs the same fixed-range
# treatment explicitly, or switching shift levels would rescale both axes
# and make magnitudes hard to compare across levels.
def _fixed(vals, pad_frac=0.10):
    lo, hi = vals.min(), vals.max()
    span = hi - lo
    pad = span * pad_frac if span else (abs(hi) * pad_frac or 1)
    return [lo - pad, hi + pad]

fig.update_xaxes(range=_fixed(scatter_df['pct_VMT']), tickformat='.1f', ticksuffix='%')
fig.update_yaxes(range=_fixed(scatter_df['pct_VHT_PER_HH']), tickformat='.1f', ticksuffix='%')
fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text='Change in VMT vs. Change in VHT/HH, by City<br><sup>City Area scenario, % of baseline</sup>', font_size=15),
    height=480, margin=dict(t=80),
    legend_title_text='County',
)
fig.show(config=CHART_CONFIG)
```

Salt Lake County's cities (Salt Lake City, West Jordan, Mill Creek) and Utah County's (Saratoga Springs, Provo, Payson) show the most movement, consistent with the [city-level results](#city-level-results) below — Saratoga Springs stands out as the largest mover on both axes.

---

## Severe Congestion: Miles Over Capacity {#severe-congestion}

VHD measures delay; this measures something funding/prioritization decisions often ask about more directly: **how many miles of roadway are actually over capacity** (volume/capacity, or V/C, greater than 1.0 — conventionally "severe congestion," roughly LOS F). A corridor can carry a lot of delay without technically being over capacity, or vice versa on a short segment, so this is a distinct, length-based read, not just another way of showing VHD. Peak V/C is the worse of AM/PM per segment; Off-Peak is the worse of MD/EV; Daily reuses the network summary's own all-day maximum.

```{python}
#| label: fig-congested-miles

cm_ft = congested_miles[(congested_miles['CO_NAME'] == 'Region') & (congested_miles['scenario_id'] != loader.BASELINE_SCENARIO)].copy()
cm_base_ft = cm_ft['base_congested_miles']
cm_ft['pct_congested_miles'] = (
    (cm_ft['delta_congested_miles'] / cm_base_ft.where(cm_base_ft > 0, 1) * 100).where(cm_base_ft > 0, 0.0)
)
cm_ft_order = cm_ft[cm_ft['period'] == 'Peak'].groupby('FTCLASS')['delta_congested_miles'].sum().sort_values().index.tolist()

def _build_congested_miles(sub, y_col='delta_congested_miles'):
    fig = px.bar(
        sub, x='FTCLASS', y=y_col, color='geography_label',
        barmode='group',
        category_orders={'FTCLASS': cm_ft_order, 'geography_label': GEO_ORDER},
        color_discrete_map=GEO_COLORS,
        labels={'FTCLASS': 'Facility Type', y_col: 'Change in Severely Congested Miles, Region', 'geography_label': 'Geography Type'},
    )
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color='#c3c2b7', line_width=1)
    fig.update_layout(xaxis_tickangle=-15)
    return fig

fig = figure_with_shift_toggle(
    cm_ft, _build_congested_miles, pct_col='pct_congested_miles',
    value_axis_title='Change in Severely Congested Miles, Region', pct_axis_title='Change in Severely Congested Miles (%), Region',
    period_col='period', default_period='Peak', period_order=PERIOD_ORDER,
)
fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text='Region-Wide Change in Miles of Severe Congestion (V/C &gt; 1.0)', font_size=15),
    height=460, margin=dict(t=80),
    legend_title_text='Geography Type',
)
fig.show(config=CHART_CONFIG)
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-congested-miles
#| output: asis

cm_primary = congested_miles[
    (congested_miles['shift_pct'] == PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT) & (congested_miles['period'] == 'Peak')
    & (congested_miles['geography_label'] == 'City Area') & (congested_miles['CO_NAME'] != 'Region')
].copy()
cm_base_primary = cm_primary['base_congested_miles']
cm_primary['pct_congested_miles'] = (
    (cm_primary['delta_congested_miles'] / cm_base_primary.where(cm_base_primary > 0, 1) * 100).where(cm_base_primary > 0, 0.0)
)
cm_primary['CO_NAME'] = pd.Categorical(cm_primary['CO_NAME'], categories=[c for c in COUNTY_ORDER if c != 'Region'], ordered=True)
cm_primary = cm_primary.sort_values(['CO_NAME', 'FTCLASS'])

def _cm_table_style(styler, caption):
    return (styler
        .set_caption(caption)
        .set_table_styles([
            {'selector': 'caption', 'props': [('font-size', '0.9em'), ('font-style', 'italic')]},
            {'selector': 'th', 'props': [('background-color', '#2a78d6'), ('color', 'white'), ('font-weight', 'bold')]},
        ])
        .hide(axis='index')
    )

cm_caption = f'City Area, {PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT}% shift, Peak period — miles of severe congestion (V/C &gt; 1.0) by county and facility type'
cm_abs = cm_primary[['CO_NAME', 'FTCLASS', 'base_congested_miles', 'delta_congested_miles']].rename(columns={
    'CO_NAME': 'County', 'FTCLASS': 'Facility Type', 'base_congested_miles': 'Miles Today (V/C>1)', 'delta_congested_miles': 'Δ Miles',
})
cm_pct = cm_primary[['CO_NAME', 'FTCLASS', 'base_congested_miles', 'pct_congested_miles']].rename(columns={
    'CO_NAME': 'County', 'FTCLASS': 'Facility Type', 'base_congested_miles': 'Miles Today (V/C>1)', 'pct_congested_miles': 'Δ Miles (%)',
})
cm_abs_styler = _cm_table_style(cm_abs.style.format({'Miles Today (V/C>1)': '{:,.1f}', 'Δ Miles': '{:+,.1f}'}), cm_caption)
cm_pct_styler = _cm_table_style(cm_pct.style.format({'Miles Today (V/C>1)': '{:,.1f}', 'Δ Miles (%)': '{:+.1f}%'}), cm_caption)
display(HTML(styled_table_with_toggle('tbl-congested-miles', cm_abs_styler, cm_pct_styler)))
```

Freeways and expressways carry the largest reduction in severely congested miles in absolute terms, matching where V/C is closest to (or over) 1.0 today; a facility type with no baseline miles over capacity shows 0% rather than an undefined change.

---

## Corridor Detail {#corridor-detail}

Volume and delay change on the region's major corridors, City Area first (the lead geography), **fixed at the 10% shift level, Peak period** (this table isn't a Plotly chart, so the shift-level/period toggle buttons used elsewhere don't apply to it — it has its own Absolute/% Change toggle instead). Corridors are ranked by City Area's VHD impact.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-corridor-city
#| output: asis

corridor_volumes_primary = corridor_volumes[(corridor_volumes['shift_pct'] == PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT) & (corridor_volumes['period'] == 'Peak')]

city_corr = corridor_volumes_primary[corridor_volumes_primary['geography_label'] == 'City Area'].sort_values('delta_DY_VHD').copy()
corr_order = city_corr['corridor_label'].tolist()
city_corr['pct_DY_Vol'] = city_corr['delta_DY_Vol'] / city_corr['base_DY_Vol'] * 100
city_corr['pct_DY_VHD'] = city_corr['delta_DY_VHD'] / city_corr['base_DY_VHD'] * 100

def _corr_table_style(styler, caption):
    return (styler
        .set_caption(caption)
        .set_table_styles([
            {'selector': 'caption', 'props': [('font-size', '0.9em'), ('font-style', 'italic')]},
            {'selector': 'th', 'props': [('background-color', '#2a78d6'), ('color', 'white'), ('font-weight', 'bold')]},
        ])
        .hide(axis='index')
    )

corr_caption = f'City Area ({PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT}% shift, Peak period) — corridor volume and delay change vs. baseline'
corr_abs = city_corr[['corridor_label', 'DY_Vol', 'delta_DY_Vol', 'DY_VHD', 'delta_DY_VHD']].rename(columns={
    'corridor_label': 'Corridor', 'DY_Vol': 'Peak Volume', 'delta_DY_Vol': 'Δ Volume',
    'DY_VHD': 'Peak VHD (hrs)', 'delta_DY_VHD': 'Δ VHD (hrs)',
})
corr_pct = city_corr[['corridor_label', 'DY_Vol', 'pct_DY_Vol', 'DY_VHD', 'pct_DY_VHD']].rename(columns={
    'corridor_label': 'Corridor', 'DY_Vol': 'Peak Volume', 'pct_DY_Vol': 'Δ Volume (%)',
    'DY_VHD': 'Peak VHD (hrs)', 'pct_DY_VHD': 'Δ VHD (%)',
})
corr_abs_styler = _corr_table_style(
    corr_abs.style.format({'Peak Volume': '{:,.0f}', 'Δ Volume': '{:+,.0f}', 'Peak VHD (hrs)': '{:,.0f}', 'Δ VHD (hrs)': '{:+,.0f}'}),
    corr_caption,
)
corr_pct_styler = _corr_table_style(
    corr_pct.style.format({'Peak Volume': '{:,.0f}', 'Δ Volume (%)': '{:+.1f}%', 'Peak VHD (hrs)': '{:,.0f}', 'Δ VHD (%)': '{:+.1f}%'}),
    corr_caption,
)
display(HTML(styled_table_with_toggle('tbl-corridor-city', corr_abs_styler, corr_pct_styler)))
```

I-15 accounts for the largest single share of City Area's regional VHD reduction, followed by Redwood Road, Mountain View Corridor, I-215, and Bangerter Highway — the region's highest-volume, most delay-prone facilities.

### Same Corridors, Medium District and Workshop Area

```{python}
#| label: fig-corridor-compare

others_all = corridor_volumes[
    (corridor_volumes['geography_label'] != 'City Area') & (corridor_volumes['scenario_id'] != loader.BASELINE_SCENARIO)
].copy()
others_all['corridor_label'] = pd.Categorical(others_all['corridor_label'], categories=corr_order, ordered=True)
others_all['pct_DY_VHD'] = others_all['delta_DY_VHD'] / others_all['base_DY_VHD'] * 100

def _build_corridor_compare(sub, y_col='delta_DY_VHD'):
    fig = px.bar(
        sub, x='corridor_label', y=y_col, color='geography_label',
        barmode='group',
        category_orders={'corridor_label': corr_order, 'geography_label': ['Medium District', 'Workshop Area']},
        color_discrete_map=GEO_COLORS,
        labels={'corridor_label': 'Corridor', y_col: 'Δ VHD (hrs)', 'geography_label': 'Geography Type'},
    )
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color='#c3c2b7', line_width=1)
    fig.update_layout(xaxis_tickangle=-45)
    return fig

fig = figure_with_shift_toggle(
    others_all, _build_corridor_compare, pct_col='pct_DY_VHD',
    value_axis_title='Δ VHD (hrs)', pct_axis_title='Δ VHD (%)',
    period_col='period', default_period='Peak', period_order=PERIOD_ORDER,
)
fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text='Corridor VHD Change — Medium District &amp; Workshop Area<br><sup>Same corridor order as City Area above</sup>', font_size=14),
    height=560, margin=dict(t=80, b=140),
)
fig.show(config=CHART_CONFIG)
```

The same corridors move in the same direction under all three geography types, at broadly similar (though not identical) magnitudes — a useful robustness check on the City Area result above.

### Freeway Corridors by County {#freeway-by-county}

Freeway/expressway corridors carry the bulk of the region's through traffic; a shared-name corridor like I-15 actually spans several counties, so here each freeway corridor is broken into the county each segment sits in instead of one blended region-wide total. City Area only (the lead geography), toggle by shift level and Peak/Off-Peak.

```{python}
#| label: fig-freeway-county

freeway_primary = corridor_volumes_primary[
    (corridor_volumes_primary['geography_label'] == 'City Area')
    & (corridor_volumes_primary['corridor_label'].isin(loader.FREEWAY_CORRIDORS))
]
freeway_order = freeway_primary.groupby('corridor_label')['delta_DY_VHD'].sum().sort_values().index.tolist()

freeway_city = freeway_by_county[freeway_by_county['geography_label'] == 'City Area'].copy()
freeway_city['corridor_label'] = pd.Categorical(freeway_city['corridor_label'], categories=freeway_order, ordered=True)
freeway_city['pct_DY_VHD'] = freeway_city['delta_DY_VHD'] / freeway_city['base_DY_VHD'] * 100

def _build_freeway_county(sub, y_col='delta_DY_VHD'):
    fig = px.bar(
        sub, x='corridor_label', y=y_col, color='CO_NAME',
        barmode='group',
        category_orders={'corridor_label': freeway_order, 'CO_NAME': COUNTY_ORDER},
        labels={'corridor_label': 'Freeway Corridor', y_col: 'Δ VHD (hrs)', 'CO_NAME': 'County'},
    )
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color='#c3c2b7', line_width=1)
    fig.update_layout(xaxis_tickangle=-20)
    return fig

fig = figure_with_shift_toggle(
    freeway_city, _build_freeway_county, pct_col='pct_DY_VHD',
    value_axis_title='Δ VHD (hrs)', pct_axis_title='Δ VHD (%)',
    period_col='period', default_period='Peak', period_order=PERIOD_ORDER,
)
fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text='Freeway Corridor VHD Change by County<br><sup>City Area scenario</sup>', font_size=15),
    height=460, margin=dict(t=80),
    legend_title_text='County',
)
fig.show(config=CHART_CONFIG)
```

I-15's reduction is concentrated in Salt Lake and Utah counties, where its own daily VHD is largest to begin with; corridors that only pass through one or two counties (Legacy Parkway, SR-201) naturally show a simpler single- or two-county picture.

### Corridor Orientation: North/South vs. East/West {#corridor-orientation}

Every named corridor above is also grouped by its predominant real-world orientation — I-215 loops around Salt Lake County and doesn't fit either category cleanly, so it gets its own "Loop" bucket rather than being forced into one.

```{python}
#| label: fig-orientation

orientation_df = corridor_orientation[corridor_orientation['scenario_id'] != loader.BASELINE_SCENARIO].copy()
orientation_df['pct_DY_VHD'] = orientation_df['delta_DY_VHD'] / orientation_df['base_DY_VHD'] * 100

def _build_orientation(sub, y_col='delta_DY_VHD'):
    fig = px.bar(
        sub, x='orientation', y=y_col, color='geography_label',
        barmode='group',
        category_orders={'orientation': ['N/S', 'E/W', 'Loop'], 'geography_label': GEO_ORDER},
        color_discrete_map=GEO_COLORS,
        labels={'orientation': 'Corridor Orientation', y_col: 'Δ VHD (hrs)', 'geography_label': 'Geography Type'},
    )
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color='#c3c2b7', line_width=1)
    return fig

fig = figure_with_shift_toggle(
    orientation_df, _build_orientation, pct_col='pct_DY_VHD',
    value_axis_title='Δ VHD (hrs)', pct_axis_title='Δ VHD (%)',
    period_col='period', default_period='Peak', period_order=PERIOD_ORDER,
)
fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text='Change in VHD by Corridor Orientation', font_size=15),
    height=440, margin=dict(t=80),
    legend_title_text='Geography Type',
)
fig.show(config=CHART_CONFIG)
```

North/south corridors (I-15, Redwood Road, State Street, Legacy Parkway, Mountain View Corridor, Bangerter Highway, US-89) carry the large majority of the named-corridor VHD reduction — expected, since the region's heaviest-volume freeways and arterials run north/south along the valley. East/west corridors and the I-215 loop see smaller, but still consistently negative, changes.

---

## Transit Ridership {#transit-ridership}

Boardings for FrontRunner, each TRAX line, and a "Bus" category aggregating every non-rail route (local, BRT, express, and premium service combined).

```{python}
#| label: fig-transit

transit_df = transit[transit['scenario_id'] != loader.BASELINE_SCENARIO].copy()
transit_df['pct_Boardings'] = transit_df['delta_Boardings'] / transit_df['base_Boardings'] * 100

def _build_transit(sub, y_col='delta_Boardings'):
    fig = px.bar(
        sub, x='line_label', y=y_col, color='geography_label',
        barmode='group',
        category_orders={'line_label': TRANSIT_LINE_ORDER, 'geography_label': GEO_ORDER},
        color_discrete_map=GEO_COLORS,
        labels={'line_label': 'Line', y_col: 'Change in Daily Boardings', 'geography_label': 'Geography Type'},
    )
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color='#c3c2b7', line_width=1)
    return fig

fig = figure_with_shift_toggle(
    transit_df, _build_transit, pct_col='pct_Boardings',
    value_axis_title='Change in Daily Boardings', pct_axis_title='Change in Daily Boardings (%)',
)
fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text='Change in Daily Boardings — Rail Lines &amp; Bus', font_size=15),
    height=440, margin=dict(t=80),
    legend_title_text='Geography Type',
)
fig.show(config=CHART_CONFIG)
```

Boardings decline slightly across every rail line and the bus aggregate, under every geography type. This is a real, non-obvious mechanical effect: bringing work trips closer to home shortens the commutes most likely to have used transit in the first place, so some riders shift to shorter drives, walks, or bikes instead. FrontRunner sees the largest absolute rail decline, consistent with it serving the longest-distance commutes; the bus aggregate's decline reflects the same effect summed across roughly 160 local/BRT/express routes.

---

## Free Time Added Back: VHT per Household {#free-time-added-back}

This metric deliberately uses **vehicle-hours of travel (VHT)**, not VHD. VHD (used in the [congestion section](#congestion-vehicle-hours-of-delay) above) only captures the delay component of travel time; VHT is the full realized travel time — free-flow time plus delay — so it also picks up the time households save simply because their trips are now *shorter*, not only because those trips hit less congestion. That's the more complete read on "time given back to households."

```{python}
#| label: fig-vht-hh

vht_df = vht_hh.copy()
vht_df['CO_NAME'] = pd.Categorical(vht_df['CO_NAME'], categories=COUNTY_ORDER, ordered=True)
vht_plot = vht_df[vht_df['scenario_id'] != loader.BASELINE_SCENARIO].copy()
vht_plot['delta_VHT_PER_HH_min'] = vht_plot['delta_VHT_PER_HH'] * 60
vht_plot['pct_VHT_PER_HH'] = vht_plot['delta_VHT_PER_HH'] / vht_plot['base_VHT_PER_HH'] * 100

def _build_vht_hh(sub, y_col='delta_VHT_PER_HH_min'):
    fig = px.bar(
        sub, x='CO_NAME', y=y_col, color='geography_label',
        barmode='group',
        category_orders={'CO_NAME': COUNTY_ORDER, 'geography_label': GEO_ORDER},
        color_discrete_map=GEO_COLORS,
        labels={'CO_NAME': 'County', y_col: 'Change in VHT / Household (min/day)', 'geography_label': 'Geography Type'},
    )
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color='#c3c2b7', line_width=1)
    return fig

fig = figure_with_shift_toggle(
    vht_plot, _build_vht_hh, pct_col='pct_VHT_PER_HH',
    value_axis_title='Change in VHT / Household (min/day)', pct_axis_title='Change in VHT / Household (%)',
    period_col='period', default_period='Peak', period_order=PERIOD_ORDER,
)
fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text='Change in Vehicle-Hours of Travel per Household<br><sup>Peak = AM+PM, Off-Peak = MD+EV</sup>', font_size=15),
    height=460, margin=dict(t=80),
    legend_title_text='Geography Type',
)
fig.show(config=CHART_CONFIG)
```

Region-wide, VHT per household falls under every geography type at both Peak and Off-Peak — a small but directionally meaningful amount of time given back to the average household, on top of whatever congestion relief accrues to specific corridors above.

---

## HBW Trip Length {#hbw-trip-length}

```{python}
#| label: fig-trip-length

tl_df = trip_length.copy()
tl_df['CO_NAME'] = pd.Categorical(tl_df['CO_NAME'], categories=COUNTY_ORDER, ordered=True)
tl_plot = tl_df[tl_df['scenario_id'] != loader.BASELINE_SCENARIO].copy()
tl_plot['pct_trip_length'] = tl_plot['delta_trip_length'] / tl_plot['base_trip_length'] * 100

def _build_trip_length(sub, y_col='delta_trip_length'):
    fig = px.bar(
        sub, x='CO_NAME', y=y_col, color='geography_label',
        barmode='group',
        category_orders={'CO_NAME': COUNTY_ORDER, 'geography_label': GEO_ORDER},
        color_discrete_map=GEO_COLORS,
        labels={'CO_NAME': 'County', y_col: 'Change in Average HBW Trip Length (miles)', 'geography_label': 'Geography Type'},
    )
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color='#c3c2b7', line_width=1)
    return fig

fig = figure_with_shift_toggle(
    tl_plot, _build_trip_length, pct_col='pct_trip_length',
    value_axis_title='Change in Average HBW Trip Length (miles)', pct_axis_title='Change in Average HBW Trip Length (%)',
)
fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text='Change in Average Home-Based-Work Trip Length<br><sup>Person-miles / trips</sup>', font_size=15),
    height=460, margin=dict(t=80),
    legend_title_text='Geography Type',
)
fig.show(config=CHART_CONFIG)
```

Average HBW trip length falls region-wide under every geography type — the most direct measure of the mechanism actually working as intended: trips that used to travel farther for work now travel a shorter distance, on average, across the whole region.

### Trip Length Distribution {#trip-length-distribution}

The average above is one number; the full distribution shows *how* the shift happens — fewer long-distance commutes, more short ones — built directly from the origin-destination trip matrix and distance skim (region-wide, all HBW trips regardless of mode, in 2-mile bins), not the production-side estimate used for the average. The baseline curve is shown alongside whichever shift level is selected, and both the trips and the distance skim are Peak/Off-Peak-correct (see the methodology note under Caveats).

```{python}
#| label: fig-trip-length-dist

td = trip_length_dist.copy()
td['scenario_label'] = td['geography_label'].where(td['geography_label'] != 'Baseline', 'Baseline')

def _build_trip_length_dist(sub):
    fig = px.line(
        sub, x='bin_label', y='share_pct', color='scenario_label',
        markers=True,
        category_orders={'bin_label': loader.DISTANCE_BIN_LABELS, 'scenario_label': ['Baseline'] + GEO_ORDER},
        color_discrete_map={**GEO_COLORS, 'Baseline': '#898781'},
        labels={'bin_label': 'HBW Trip Length', 'share_pct': 'Share of Trips (%)', 'scenario_label': 'Scenario'},
    )
    fig.update_traces(selector=dict(name='Baseline'), line=dict(color='black', dash='dash', width=2), marker=dict(color='black'))
    return fig

fig = figure_with_shift_toggle(
    td, _build_trip_length_dist, always_include=[0],
    period_col='period', default_period='Peak', period_order=PERIOD_ORDER,
)
fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text='HBW Trip Length Distribution, Baseline vs. Shift<br><sup>Region-wide, weighted by O-D trip volume, 2-mile bins</sup>', font_size=15),
    height=460, margin=dict(t=80),
    legend_title_text='Scenario',
)
fig.update_xaxes(range=[-0.5, loader.DISTANCE_BIN_EDGES.index(30) - 0.5])
fig.update_yaxes(range=[0, 25])
fig.show(config=CHART_CONFIG)
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis

rows = []
for geo in GEO_ORDER:
    v = trip_length_avg[
        (trip_length_avg['geography_label'] == geo) & (trip_length_avg['shift_pct'] == PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT)
        & (trip_length_avg['period'] == 'Peak')
    ]['delta_weighted_avg_trip_length']
    if len(v):
        rows.append(f"- **{geo}**: weighted-average trip length changes by **{v.iloc[0]:+.2f} miles**.")
display(Markdown(
    f"Cross-check against the production-side average above, **fixed at the {PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT}% shift level, "
    "Peak period** (independent methodology — full O-D skim × trip volume, not PMT/trips):\n\n" + "\n".join(rows)
))
```

---

## What This Means for Specific Cities {#city-level-results}

The City Area scenario redistributes every city's own outbound HBW trips simultaneously — here's what that looks like for ten specific cities picked to span the region's size and geography: **Ogden, Layton, Salt Lake City, West Jordan, Saratoga Springs, Roy, North Salt Lake, Mill Creek, Provo, and Payson**. This section is only meaningful under the City Area geography type, since that's the one whose redistribution boundaries are these same cities; Medium District and Workshop Area use different (larger, differently-shaped) target areas and aren't shown here.

```{python}
#| label: fig-city-trip-length

city_df = city_results[city_results['scenario_id'] != loader.BASELINE_SCENARIO].copy()
city_df['pct_trip_length'] = city_df['delta_trip_length'] / city_df['base_trip_length'] * 100
city_df['delta_VHT_PER_HH_min'] = city_df['delta_VHT_PER_HH'] * 60
city_df['pct_VHT_PER_HH'] = city_df['delta_VHT_PER_HH'] / city_df['base_VHT_PER_HH'] * 100

def _build_city_trip_length(sub, y_col='delta_trip_length'):
    fig = px.bar(
        sub, x='city_label', y=y_col,
        category_orders={'city_label': CITY_ORDER},
        color_discrete_sequence=[GEO_COLORS['City Area']],
        labels={'city_label': '', y_col: 'Δ Average HBW Trip Length (mi)'},
    )
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color='#c3c2b7', line_width=1)
    return fig

fig = figure_with_shift_toggle(
    city_df, _build_city_trip_length, pct_col='pct_trip_length',
    value_axis_title='Δ Average HBW Trip Length (mi)', pct_axis_title='Δ Average HBW Trip Length (%)',
)
fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text='Change in Average HBW Trip Length by City<br><sup>City Area scenario, households/trips originating in each city</sup>', font_size=15),
    height=440, margin=dict(t=80),
)
fig.show(config=CHART_CONFIG)
```

```{python}
#| label: fig-city-vht

def _build_city_vht(sub, y_col='delta_VHT_PER_HH_min'):
    fig = px.bar(
        sub, x='city_label', y=y_col,
        category_orders={'city_label': CITY_ORDER},
        color_discrete_sequence=[GEO_COLORS['City Area']],
        labels={'city_label': '', y_col: 'Δ Peak HBW VHT / Household (min/day)'},
    )
    fig.add_hline(y=0, line_color='#c3c2b7', line_width=1)
    return fig

fig = figure_with_shift_toggle(
    city_df, _build_city_vht, pct_col='pct_VHT_PER_HH',
    value_axis_title='Δ Peak HBW VHT / Household (min/day)', pct_axis_title='Δ Peak HBW VHT / Household (%)',
)
fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text='Change in Peak HBW Travel Time per Household by City<br><sup>City Area scenario</sup>', font_size=15),
    height=440, margin=dict(t=80),
)
fig.show(config=CHART_CONFIG)
```

```{python}
#| label: tbl-city
#| output: asis

city_primary = city_results[
    (city_results['scenario_id'] != loader.BASELINE_SCENARIO) & (city_results['shift_pct'] == PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT)
].sort_values('delta_trip_length').copy()
city_primary['delta_VHT_PER_HH_min'] = city_primary['delta_VHT_PER_HH'] * 60
city_primary['pct_VMT'] = city_primary['delta_VMT'] / city_primary['base_VMT'] * 100
city_primary['pct_VHT_PER_HH'] = city_primary['delta_VHT_PER_HH'] / city_primary['base_VHT_PER_HH'] * 100
city_primary['pct_trip_length'] = city_primary['delta_trip_length'] / city_primary['base_trip_length'] * 100

def _city_table_style(styler, caption):
    return (styler
        .set_caption(caption)
        .set_table_styles([
            {'selector': 'caption', 'props': [('font-size', '0.9em'), ('font-style', 'italic')]},
            {'selector': 'th', 'props': [('background-color', '#2a78d6'), ('color', 'white'), ('font-weight', 'bold')]},
        ])
        .hide(axis='index')
    )

city_caption = f'City Area, {PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT}% shift — production-side change by city (fixed at {PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT}%)'
city_abs = city_primary[['city_label', 'delta_VMT', 'delta_VHT_PER_HH_min', 'delta_trip_length']].rename(columns={
    'city_label': 'City', 'delta_VMT': 'Δ Daily HBW VMT', 'delta_VHT_PER_HH_min': 'Δ Peak VHT/HH (min)',
    'delta_trip_length': 'Δ Trip Length (mi)',
})
city_pct = city_primary[['city_label', 'pct_VMT', 'pct_VHT_PER_HH', 'pct_trip_length']].rename(columns={
    'city_label': 'City', 'pct_VMT': 'Δ Daily HBW VMT (%)', 'pct_VHT_PER_HH': 'Δ Peak VHT/HH (%)',
    'pct_trip_length': 'Δ Trip Length (%)',
})
city_abs_styler = _city_table_style(
    city_abs.style.format({'Δ Daily HBW VMT': '{:+,.0f}', 'Δ Peak VHT/HH (min)': '{:+.2f}', 'Δ Trip Length (mi)': '{:+.2f}'}),
    city_caption,
)
city_pct_styler = _city_table_style(
    city_pct.style.format({'Δ Daily HBW VMT (%)': '{:+.1f}%', 'Δ Peak VHT/HH (%)': '{:+.1f}%', 'Δ Trip Length (%)': '{:+.1f}%'}),
    city_caption,
)
display(HTML(styled_table_with_toggle('tbl-city', city_abs_styler, city_pct_styler)))
```

Saratoga Springs sees the largest trip-length and time reduction of the five, plausibly because it's the newest/most rapidly-growing of the five with the most outbound commuting to begin with, so a proportional shift back in has more room to matter. Salt Lake City sees the smallest change — unsurprising, since it already has the region's largest, most diverse local job base, so a smaller share of its residents' HBW trips end up leaving the city in the first place.

---

## Mode Share {#mode-share}

```{python}
#| label: fig-mode-share

MODE_GROUPS = {
    'Auto': ['Auto', 'RegAuto', 'IMZD2D'],
    'Transit': ['LCL', 'COR', 'BRT', 'EXP', 'LRT', 'CRT'],
    'Walk': ['Walk'], 'Bike': ['Bike'],
}
mode_to_group = {m: g for g, ms in MODE_GROUPS.items() for m in ms}

hbw = mode_share[mode_share['TRIPPURP'] == 'HBW'].copy()
hbw['group'] = hbw['MODE'].map(mode_to_group).fillna('Other')
hbw_grp = hbw.groupby(['scenario_id', 'geography_label', 'shift_pct', 'group'], as_index=False)['share_pct'].sum()
hbw_grp = hbw_grp[~hbw_grp['group'].isin(['Auto', 'Other'])]

def _build_mode_share(sub):
    fig = px.bar(
        sub, x='group', y='share_pct', color='geography_label',
        barmode='group',
        category_orders={'group': ['Transit', 'Walk', 'Bike'], 'geography_label': ['Baseline'] + GEO_ORDER},
        color_discrete_map={**GEO_COLORS, 'Baseline': '#898781'},
        labels={'group': 'Mode', 'share_pct': 'Share of HBW Trips (%)', 'geography_label': 'Scenario'},
    )
    return fig

fig = figure_with_shift_toggle(hbw_grp, _build_mode_share, always_include=[0])
fig.update_layout(
    title=dict(text='Home-Based-Work Mode Share, Baseline vs. Shift<br><sup>Regionwide, peak + off-peak</sup>', font_size=15),
    height=460, margin=dict(t=80),
    legend_title_text='Scenario',
)
fig.show(config=CHART_CONFIG)
```

HBW mode share barely moves at any shift level — consistent with the mechanism only touching trip *destinations*, not the mode choice model's own logic. The small transit decline visible here matches the ridership drop discussed above.

---

## Cross-Scale Comparison {#cross-scale-comparison}

All three geography types, side by side, **fixed at the 10% shift level, Peak period** (not shift/period-toggle-enabled, but has its own Absolute/% Change toggle below). Region-wide figures.

```{python}
#| label: tbl-cross-scale
#| output: asis

vmt_vhd_primary = vmt_vhd[(vmt_vhd['shift_pct'] == PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT) & (vmt_vhd['period'] == 'Peak')]
vht_hh_primary = vht_hh[(vht_hh['shift_pct'] == PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT) & (vht_hh['period'] == 'Peak')]
trip_length_primary = trip_length[trip_length['shift_pct'] == PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT]
transit_primary = transit[transit['shift_pct'] == PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT]

abs_rows, pct_rows = [], []
for geo in GEO_ORDER:
    vhd_row = vmt_vhd_primary[(vmt_vhd_primary['CO_NAME'] == 'Region') & (vmt_vhd_primary['geography_label'] == geo)]
    vhd, base_vhd = vhd_row['delta_DY_VHD'].sum(), vhd_row['base_DY_VHD'].sum()
    vmt, base_vmt = vhd_row['delta_DY_VMT'].sum(), vhd_row['base_DY_VMT'].sum()
    hh_row = vht_hh_primary[(vht_hh_primary['CO_NAME'] == 'Region') & (vht_hh_primary['geography_label'] == geo)].iloc[0]
    hh_min, base_hh_min = hh_row['delta_VHT_PER_HH'] * 60, hh_row['base_VHT_PER_HH'] * 60
    tl_row = trip_length_primary[(trip_length_primary['CO_NAME'] == 'Region') & (trip_length_primary['geography_label'] == geo)].iloc[0]
    tl, base_tl = tl_row['delta_trip_length'], tl_row['base_trip_length']
    board_row = transit_primary[transit_primary['geography_label'] == geo]
    board, base_board = board_row['delta_Boardings'].sum(), board_row['base_Boardings'].sum()
    abs_rows.append({
        'Geography Type': geo, 'Δ VHD (hrs)': vhd, 'Δ VMT (mi)': vmt,
        'Δ Peak VHT/HH (min)': hh_min, 'Δ HBW Trip Length (mi)': tl, 'Δ Transit Boardings': board,
    })
    pct_rows.append({
        'Geography Type': geo, 'Δ VHD (%)': vhd / base_vhd * 100, 'Δ VMT (%)': vmt / base_vmt * 100,
        'Δ Peak VHT/HH (%)': hh_min / base_hh_min * 100, 'Δ HBW Trip Length (%)': tl / base_tl * 100,
        'Δ Transit Boardings (%)': board / base_board * 100,
    })

cross_caption = f'Region-wide change vs. baseline, {PRIMARY_SHIFT_PCT}% shift level, Peak period'

def _cross_table_style(styler):
    return (styler
        .set_caption(cross_caption)
        .set_table_styles([
            {'selector': 'caption', 'props': [('font-size', '0.9em'), ('font-style', 'italic')]},
            {'selector': 'th', 'props': [('background-color', '#2a78d6'), ('color', 'white'), ('font-weight', 'bold')]},
        ])
        .hide(axis='index')
    )

cross_abs_styler = _cross_table_style(pd.DataFrame(abs_rows).style.format({
    'Δ VHD (hrs)': '{:+,.0f}', 'Δ VMT (mi)': '{:+,.0f}',
    'Δ Peak VHT/HH (min)': '{:+.2f}', 'Δ HBW Trip Length (mi)': '{:+.3f}',
    'Δ Transit Boardings': '{:+,.0f}',
}))
cross_pct_styler = _cross_table_style(pd.DataFrame(pct_rows).style.format({
    'Δ VHD (%)': '{:+.1f}%', 'Δ VMT (%)': '{:+.1f}%',
    'Δ Peak VHT/HH (%)': '{:+.1f}%', 'Δ HBW Trip Length (%)': '{:+.1f}%',
    'Δ Transit Boardings (%)': '{:+.1f}%',
}))
display(HTML(styled_table_with_toggle('tbl-cross-scale', cross_abs_styler, cross_pct_styler)))
```

At the 10% shift level, City Area and Medium District produce broadly similar regional effects; Workshop Area's are consistently the smallest of the three — plausible given it's the coarsest geography (only 8 areas region-wide, so "outside the area" captures fewer actually-nearby destinations than City Area or Medium District would).

---

## Caveats and Interpretation {#caveats-and-interpretation}

This is a **sketch-level, order-of-magnitude test**, not a land-use or economic forecast. Specifically:

- **Not a real land-use or economic forecast.** No businesses actually relocate, no zoning changes, no job growth is simulated — the trip table is manually forced to behave as if that happened.
- **Feasibility is not checked.** The model does not confirm the target area could physically hold the added jobs (land, buildings, parking).
- **It's a sketch, not a precise estimate.** Trips are manually reallocated using today's existing destination pattern rather than letting the model's own distribution logic decide — results are order-of-magnitude, not precise numbers.
- **Only home-based-work trips are touched.** Other trip purposes (shopping, school, etc.) are unchanged.
- **No second-order effects.** Housing costs, people moving closer to new jobs, or businesses responding to changed traffic patterns are not captured.
- **Three shift levels test sensitivity to size, not to method.** 5%, 10%, and 25% (once all are run) all use the same proportional redistribution logic — this is not a test of alternative ways to choose which trips move or where they land. The toggle buttons let you compare magnitudes across levels, not alternative mechanisms.
- **Corridor orientation and freeway groupings are a manual simplification.** Each named corridor is assigned one predominant orientation/classification for this sketch-level rollup, not a segment-by-segment geometric analysis — see the Appendix.

---

## Appendix {#appendix}

**Mechanism.** Implemented in `run_sets/bring-work-trips-closer-to-home/scripts/redistribute_hbw_trips.py`, wired into the `__HailMary_1Subfolder_closer.s` driver script: the HBW destination-choice matrix is converted to OMX, redistributed using a TAZ-level geography lookup (`CITY_UGRC` / `DISTMED` / `CITYGRP` fields on `WFv1000_TAZ.dbf`), then converted back — applied before mode choice.

**Model version and vintage.** `v10.0.0-beta.1-closer-to-home`, 2027 RTP adoption-year network and land use, baseline Control Center `_ControlCenter - OY_2027.block`.

**Scenario design.** See [How the Test Works](#how-the-test-works) above; full config in `run_sets/bring-work-trips-closer-to-home/run_set.yaml` and `scenarios/`.

**A note on run status.** Closer01–06's `run_metadata.json` originally recorded these runs as `failed` (Cube Voyager's cluster-shutdown step returned a nonzero exit code after the model had already completed and written all outputs) — verified by inspecting each run's Voyager print log, which shows every step completing and `TOTAL MODEL RUN TIME` logged before the exit-code issue. Outputs were re-curated via `tdmruns import-manual-run`, which recorded a clean `success` status against the same underlying model run.

**Trips redistributed (Plain-Language Takeaways).** `report_loader.py`'s `compute_city_area_shift_trips()` estimates how many baseline daily HBW trips start inside a City Area but end outside it (region-wide, across all ~80 City Area groups at once), then applies the shift level directly to that count. This requires knowing which TAZID each row/column of the baseline HBW trip matrix (an OMX file) corresponds to — the curated OMX carries no explicit zone mapping (`list_mappings()` returns empty), so the function assumes the standard convention: index 0..N-1 is TAZID 1..N in ascending order for the model's 3,562 internal TAZs, with the matrix's remaining 67 rows/columns (it's 3,629×3,629) being external stations or special generator zones outside any City Area. This assumption has **not** been independently verified against the model's own zone-numbering documentation — treat the trips-redistributed figure as an order-of-magnitude estimate, not an exact count.

**Shift-level toggle.** Most charts pre-build every available shift level's traces and use Plotly's `updatemenus` (client-side show/hide buttons, rendered as a vertical column to the right of the chart) to switch between them, since a static Quarto render has no backend to re-run Python on a click. The currently-selected button in each group is styled with a dark navy background and white text (every other option light teal/navy text) so the active choice is unambiguous — Plotly's own `updatemenus` have no built-in active-button highlighting, so each visible "button" is actually its own single-button menu, restyled on click and padded to a shared width (the widest label used anywhere, "Medium District") so buttons read as one consistent control; see `reports/chart_utils.py`'s `_col_menus`/`_button_style`/`_button_pad`. Most VMT/VHD/VHT charts also carry a Peak/Off-Peak/Daily group (same show/hide mechanism, applied to a second, independent dimension) and a third, independent group of buttons ("Absolute" / "% Change") to view each value as a percent of its own baseline instead of a raw unit change. The Absolute/% Change group restyles trace *values* rather than trace *visibility*, so it composes freely with the other groups. Shift level, period, and (where present) geography type all restyle visibility, however — clicking one resets the others to their default (Peak, 10%, City Area), since a static page has no way for one button to know what another button currently has selected; see `figure_with_shift_toggle`'s docstring. Bar charts also show a value label on every bar and round hover text to 3 significant figures (rather than a fixed decimal count, so it reads sensibly across metrics spanning sub-1 to five-digit magnitudes). A handful of tables and narrative figures are pandas-styled dataframes, not Plotly figures, so this same mechanism doesn't apply to them directly — those stay fixed at the 10% shift level, Peak period, and say so explicitly, but get their own Absolute/% Change toggle instead (`chart_utils.styled_table_with_toggle`, plain HTML/CSS/JS show-hide, styled to match).

**Freeway/orientation groupings.** `run_sets/bring-work-trips-closer-to-home/report_loader.py`'s `FREEWAY_CORRIDORS` and `CORRIDOR_ORIENTATION` are a manual, corridor-level classification (not a per-segment geometric analysis): freeway/expressway status matches the scoping memo's own grouping and is consistent with each corridor's dominant `FTCLASS` in `Summary_SEGID.csv`; orientation (N/S vs. E/W) reflects each corridor's predominant real-world heading, except I-215, which loops around Salt Lake County and gets its own "Loop" category rather than being forced into either.

**City-level results.** The ten cities (`TARGET_CITIES` in `report_loader.py`) were picked to span the region's size range and geography, from a small, newer, rapidly-growing city (Saratoga Springs) to the region's largest, most job-dense city (Salt Lake City), with a mix of established (Ogden, Layton), mid-size (West Jordan, Provo), and smaller suburban (Roy, North Salt Lake, Mill Creek, Payson) cities in between, spanning Weber, Davis, Salt Lake, and Utah counties. Matched to `WFv1000_TAZ.dbf`'s `CITY_UGRC` codes (`OGD`/`LAY`/`SLC`/`WJC`/`SAR`/`ROY`/`NSL`/`MLC`/`PVO`/`PAY`, confirmed against `tdm/1_Inputs/1_TAZ/Districts/City.dbf`'s `CITY_NAME` field); only meaningful under the City Area scenarios, since that's the geography type whose redistribution is actually scoped by these same city boundaries. Each city sits entirely within one county (also confirmed against `WFv1000_TAZ.dbf`), so `build_city_results()` can carry a single `CO_NAME` per city for county-colored city-level charts (e.g. the [VMT vs. VHT/HH scatter](#vmt-vs-vhd)). There's no per-city VHD: VHD comes from `Summary_SEGID`, a link-level network summary carrying a county code (`CO_FIPS`) per link, not a city one — attributing delay to individual cities would need a new link-to-city spatial join that doesn't exist yet, so city-level charts pair VMT against VHT/household (both already computed per-city from TAZ-level production summaries) instead of VHD.

**Bus aggregate.** `_transit_brding_summary_route.csv`'s own `Mode` field distinguishes TRAX/streetcar rail (7) and FrontRunner commuter rail (8) from everything else (4 = local bus, 5 = BRT, 6 = express, 9 = premium/other) — the "Bus (all non-rail)" category sums every route in that latter group (roughly 160 individual route codes) rather than enumerating them by name.

**Corridor definitions.** `run_sets/bring-work-trips-closer-to-home/inputs/corridor_segid_crosswalk.csv`, built from the network's link file (`tdm/1_Inputs/3_Highway/GIS/WFv1000_MasterNet_*.dbf`) with manual keyword matching and priority tie-breaking at interchange segments — see `REPORT_PLAN.md` for the full method.

**Trip length distribution data.** The distribution above (and its weighted-average cross-check) is built from curated matrix extracts, not from a summary CSV, and is Peak/Off-Peak-correct: Peak rows pair the `motor`/`nonmotor` tables of the Peak-only HBW trip matrix (`4_ModeChoice/2_DetailedTripMatrices/HBW_trips_allsegs_Pk.mtx`) against a Peak distance skim (the unweighted average of the `GP_Dist` tables of the AM and PM final skims, `5_AssignHwy/5_FinalNetSkims/Skm_AM.mtx`/`Skm_PM.mtx`); Off-Peak rows pair the Ok trip matrix against the MD/EV skims' averaged `GP_Dist`. Averaging is unweighted because HBW trips are only ever split this coarsely (Peak/Off-Peak), not further into AM/MD/PM/EV, so there's no period-specific trip volume to weight the distance average by — a reasonable simplification since AM/MD/PM/EV/DY `GP_Dist` means differ by well under 1% of each other in practice (distance is a largely static network attribute along whichever path was assigned). An earlier version of this chart used a single all-day (`Skm_DY.mtx`) distance skim applied uniformly to the combined Peak+Off-Peak (`HBW_trips_allsegs_pkok.mtx`) trip matrix, mismatching trips against a distance that wasn't specific to when they actually travel. All of these matrices are several hundred MB in full, curated down to small single/two-table OMX files via the `outputs.include` `matrix:`/`tabs:` entry type (see `run_set.yaml`), which invokes Cube Voyager's CONVERTMAT at curation time. The trip matrices' raw values are stored at 100x scale (confirmed empirically against `_ZoneSummary_TripsByMode.csv`'s trusted total, within 0.001%) and divided back down in `report_loader.py`. Distance-skim cells at Cube's `NOACCESS` sentinel (9999, unconnected zone pairs) are excluded before binning, now in 2-mile bins.
